Message Gateway 

The Message Gateway has these functions: 

• Barrier messages for thread-to-thread synchronization within a thread group. 

• Event messages (Monitor, Wait, and Signal) for thread-to-thread signaling across GPU. 

End of Thread messages to exit the GPGPU threads. 

Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21   711 



    

 

Messages  

This section describes the Message Gateway messages. 

Message Descriptor 

The following message descriptor applies to all messages supported by Message Gateway. 

Bits Description 

19 Header Present. This bit must be 0 for all Message Gateway messages. 

18:17 Ignored. 

16:3 Reserved: MBZ 

2:0 
SubFuncID. Identify the supported sub-functions by Message Gateway. Encodings are: 

Value Name Description 

001b SignalEvent 
Sends an event to all threads that are monitoring that Event ID 

010b MonitorEvent Gateway will remember if this Event ID occurs 

011b MonitorNoEvent Gateway will no longer record any Events for sending thread. 

100b BarrierMsg Record an additional thread reaching the barrier 

110b WaitForEvent Send a writeback if Event ID occured after MonitorEvent 

All other encodings Reserved. 

 

BarrierMsg Message 

The BarrierMsg message gives the ability for multiple threads to synchronize their progress. This is useful 
when there are data shared between threads. The message consists of a single 256-bit message payload. 

Upon receiving one such message, Message Gateway increments the Barrier counter and marks the 
Barrier requester thread. There is no immediate response from the Message Gateway when the 
incremented counter is not equal to the terminating thread count. When the incremented counter value 
does equal the Barrier Thread Count, Message Gateway sends a response back to all the Barrier 
requester threads and resets the Barrier count to zero. 

712   Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21 



  
  

 

 

Programming Note 

Context: BarrierMsg Message 

The Message Gateway assumes that the barrier ID sent in barrier message payload is valid and was allocated by 
TSG. In the event of a programming error specifying an invalid barrier ID, the results are undefined, and may cause 
the Message Gateway to stop responding to barrier messages for any thread that it services. 

Message Payload 

DWord Bits Description 

M0.5 31:0 Ignored 

M0.4 31:0 Ignored 

M0.3 31:0 Predicate Mask. This field has a bit set per SIMD channel that passes the predicate. For SIMD8 and 
SIMD16 the rest of the bits must be 0. This field is ignored for non-predicated barriers. 

M0.2 31 Ignored 

30:24 
BarrierID. This field indicates which barrier state is updated. 

Format: U7 

Range = [0, 63] 

23 
Predicate Mask Enable. This bit indicates that the barrier is a predicated barrier and the SIMD 
channels passing the predicate should be summed. All threads sending this message to the same 
barrier should have an identical value for this field, and must specify a response length of 1 for the 
predicate sum response. Note that Global Barriers must not have the Predicate Mask Enable bit set. 

Programming Note: This control is intended only for GPGPU or Media threads. This control must 
not be set if the barrier is for a Hull Shader thread. 

Format: Enable 

22:16 Ignored 

15 
Barrier Count Enable. Allows the message to reprogram the terminating barrier count. If set, the 
stored value of  the terminating barrier count is set to the value of Barrier Count field (below), and 
used for this barrier operation. If clear, the stored value of the terminating barrier count is not 
modified and the stored value is used for this barrier operation. 

Programming Note: This control is intended only for Hull Shader threads. This control must not be 
set if the barrier is allocated by a GPGPU or Media thread. 

Format: Enable 

14:8 
Barrier Count. If Barrier Count Enable is set, this field specifies the terminating barrier count. 
Otherwise this field is ignored. All threads that belong to a single barrier must deliver the same 
value for this field for a particular barrier iteration. 

Format: U7 

Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21   713 



    

 

DWord Bits Description 

7:0 Ignored 

M0.1 31:0 Ignored 

M0.0 31:4 Ignored 

Writeback Message to Requester Thread 

The writeback message is sent only if the AckReq bit in the message descriptor is set. 

Programming Note 

Context: Writeback Message to Requester Thread 

AckReq must not be used with predicate barriers, because the predicate barrier response is sent to the GRF 
writeback location instead of the Ack. 

 

DWord Bits Description 

W0.7:1  Reserved (not overwritten) 

W0.0 31:20 Reserved 

19:16 Shared Function ID. Contains the message gateway’s shared function ID. 

15:3 Reserved 

2:0 
Error Code 

000: Successful. No Error (Normal). 

Other encodings are reserved. 

Broadcast Writeback Message 

Description 

When the count for a Barrier reaches Barrier.Count, the Message Gateway sends the notification bit N0 to each 
EU/Thread that reached the barrier. A Barrier Return Byte is not sent. 

This response message is sent back only if the Gateway Barrier Message specifies that this is a predicated barrier. 
This response is written to the GRF writeback location, and the response length specified in the send message to 
the EU must be 1. 

 

DWord Bits Description 

W0.7:1  Reserved (not overwritten) 

31:16 Reserved (not overwritten) 
W0.0 

15:0 Predicated Barrier Mask Sum. This field is a sum of the predicate mask bits sent by each thread. 
This field (and the DW containing it) is not written if the barrier is not marked as a predicated 
barrier. The kernel should compare this field to 0 for the predicated OR function and compare it to 
the workgroup size for the predicated AND function. 

714   Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21 



  
  

 

MonitorEvent 

The MonitorEvent message indicates that the Gateway should remember any events of the specified 
EventID for the messaging thread. No writeback message is sent until a WaitForEvent message arrives, 
however. Only one event can be monitored at a time per thread, so a new MonitorEvent message will 
replace the event being monitored by the sending thread with the new EventID. An event recorded for 
the thread by a previous MonitorEvent is cleared when a new MonitorEvent comes in. 

Message Payload 

DWord Bits Description 

M0.7 31:0 Ignored 

M0.6 31:0 Ignored 

M0.5 31:0 Ignored 

M0.4 31:0 Ignored 

M0.3 31:0 Ignored 

M0.2 31:0 Ignored 

M0.1 31:0 Ignored 

M0.0 31:24 Ignored 

23:0 EventID - Indicates the ID of the event to be monitored. 

WaitForEvent 

The WaitForEvent message causes the Gateway to send a writeback message to the thread when a 
monitored event occurs, or to send the writeback immediately if the event occurred before the 
WaitForEvent and after a MonitorEvent. A timeout value is supplied to cause the Gateway to send the 
writeback after the timeout occurs. Timeout always happens during pre-emption. 

Programming Note 

The return of a WaitForEvent writeback message does not mean that that the monitored event occurred. A timeout 
or preemption may have occurred, or the signal could have been sent by a previous signaling phase, or possibly 
even by another program that is using the same event number. 

Therefore, each monitor event should be used in combination with a global memory location that is written and 
read with atomic messages. That memory location records the signal, and the monitor event becomes a way to 
know when to poll the global memory location to see if the event has occurred. 

Message Payload 

DWord Bits Description 

M0.7 31:0 
Ignored 

M0.6 31:0 Ignored 

M0.5 31:0 Ignored 

Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21   715 



    

 

DWord Bits Description 

M0.4 31:0 Ignored 

M0.3 31:0 Ignored 

M0.2 31:0 Ignored 

M0.1 31:0 Ignored 

M0.0 31:10 Ignored 

9:0 Timeout-  The amount of time GW should wait before sending a writeback message. This value is in 
terms of 1024 clocks, so with a 1Ghz clock it would be approximately in terms of uS. 0 and 1 are 
illegal values, since the actual timeout time can be short by up to 1 increment of the timeout value. 

Writeback Message to Requester Thread 

DWord Bit Description 

R0.7 31:0 Ignored 

R0.6 31:0 Ignored 

R0.5 31:0 Ignored 

R0.4 31:0 Ignored 

R0.3 31:0 Ignored 

R0.2 31:0 Ignored 

R0.1 31:0 Ignored 

R0.0 31:1 Ignored 

 0 Event Occurred - This bit is 1 if the event occurred, or 0 if a timeout occurred. 

 

Programming Note 

Timing race conditions exist between one thread signaling an event, and another thread waiting for the event. It is 
possible that the WaitForEvent times out even when a signal has been sent. It is also possible that a WaitForEvent 
receives an EventOccurred from a prior signaling event that wasn't cleared out. 

The correct programming model for Monitors uses atomic operations on a memory location shared by the threads 
for synchronization and monitors to reduce polling overhead on the memory location. The signaling thread first 
specifies the event with an atomic operation and then signals the event. The receiving thread waits for the event 
(which might be correct, might be a timeout or might be an old signal), and then verifies the event by checking the 
shared memory location. 

SignalEvent 

This message indicates to the rest of the system that the specified event has occurred. This event will be 
recorded for all threads monitoring it, and messages will be sent to all threads with an outstanding 
WaitForEvent. 

716   Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21 



  
  

 

Message Payload 

DWord Bits Description 

M0.7 31:0 
Ignored 

M0.6 31:0 Ignored 

M0.5 31:0 Ignored 

M0.4 31:0 Ignored 

M0.3 31:0 Ignored 

M0.2 31:0 Ignored 

M0.1 31:0 Ignored 

M0.0 31:24 Ignored 

23:0 EventID - Indicates the ID of the event to be signaled. 

MonitorNoEvent 

This message indicates that the thread no longer wishes to monitor any events. The EOT message at the 
end of the thread does the same thing, so this message is optional for most usages. It is not needed 
when switching between different events - just a new MonitorEvent with the new Event ID suffices. 

Message Payload 

DWord Bits Description 

M0.7 31:0 
Ignored 

M0.6 31:0 Ignored 

M0.5 31:0 Ignored 

M0.4 31:0 Ignored 

M0.3 31:0 Ignored 

M0.2 31:0 Ignored 

M0.1 31:0 Ignored 

M0.0 31:0 Ignored 

Barrier Messages 

Barriers are used to synchronize the execution of two or more concurrently running threads in a 
threadgroup. 

Each thread executes the barrier in two parts: 

• send a signal_barrier message to Gateway port 

• use sync.bar instruction to wait for a barrier completion notification from Gateway port 

Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21   717 



    

 

When all the threads sharing the same barrier have signaled the barrier, then the Gateway port notifies 
the threads that the barrier is complete. 

The barrier is marked inactive when it is allocated, and whenever a barrier completion notification is sent. 

A barrier is allocated when a threadgroup is dispatched, and released when all the threads in the 
threadgroup exit. A thread that signals a barrier must wait for the completion notification before the 
thread exits. 

Event Messages 

Event Monitors are used by threads to be notified of an event signaled by another running threads in the 
GPU. 

When a thread sends a signal_event message to Gateway port, the event is broadcast to all running 
threads in the GPU that have previously requested to be notified of the event. 

To receive an event, a thread: 

1. Requests to be notified of an Event by sending a monitor_event message to the Gateway port. This 
registers the thread as expecting the event. 

2. Waits until the event is signaled (or a timeout occurs) by sending a wait_event message to the 
Gateway port. 

3. Checks a memory-based semaphore to ensure that the signal was received. 

Events are not, by themselves, a thread synchronization mechanism. Typically, independently running 
threads synchronize using atomic operations on memory-based semaphores. Events are a mechanism to 
reduce polling operations on memory-based semaphores. 

Sample code to send and receive events with a semaphore: 

Signaling Thread Receiving Thread 

extern int semaphore = 0; extern int semaphore; 

// do work, produce results ... monitor_event(&semaphore); 

atomic_store(&semaphore, 1); while ((atomic_cmpxchg(&semaphore, 1, 0) == 0) { 

signal_event(&semaphore);     wait_event( ); 

} 

monitor_no_event( ); 

// consume other thread's results ... 

When a thread exits, it is automatically unregistered from any events (implicit monitor_no_event). 

718   Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21 



  
  

 

End of Thread (EOT) Message 

A thread exits by executing a send instruction with the EOT bit set. Only a small number of send 
messages support EOT. The Gateway EOT message is one of those few messages. (Which message to use 
for a thread depends on which fixed function was used to dispatch the thread.)  

The Gateway EOT message removes the barrier and event tracking for the thread, and decrements the 
use count on the thread's barriers and shared local memory. When the use count for a barrier or shared 
local memory reaches zero, then that resource is available for allocation by another threadgroup. 

Doc Ref # IHD-OS-TGL-Vol 9-12.21   719
